hair.value: Golden-blonde, with streaks of white. Full beard

system.biography:
Torsten stands five feet eleven inches, built broad across the shoulders with the heavy musculature of a working smith. At forty-four, his body shows the accumulated impact of decades at the forge - muscular forearms with prominent veins and tendons, large calloused hands scarred from metalwork and magic, a silvery burn scar running along his right forearm from an early laboratory accident. His skin is sun-darkened from maritime travel, weathered beyond his years from outdoor work at shipyards. His nose has been broken at least twice, giving his face a blunt, practical cast rather than classical handsomeness.

Golden-blonde hair falls to his jaw, shot through with premature streaks of pure white that began appearing in his late twenties. He wears it loose when working, tied back for precision tasks. His full beard, similarly streaked with silver, is kept practical rather than ornamental. Grey-blue eyes like North Sea waters assess problems with methodical patience..

His presence projects competence rather than charm. He moves with the deliberate economy of someone accustomed to working with dangerous materials. He dresses in heavy wool tunics (dark grey or brown) beneath well-worn leather aprons, practical layers that protect from forge heat and allow freedom of movement. Minimal adornment - a simple bronze ring on his left hand, occasionally a pendant tucked beneath his tunic, nothing that could catch in machinery or interfere with spellcasting.

His voice carries the lilting cadence of Norwegian overlaid with years of speaking High German and Latin. He speaks precisely, choosing words with the same care he applies to metalwork, and grows animated only when discussing technical challenges or design problems. When he's crafting enchantments, observers report an almost meditative focus - the restlessness vanishes entirely, replaced by absolute concentration on the work.

Born: 1161, Schleswig (border territory between Denmark and the Holy Roman Empire)

House: Verditius

Former Covenant: Triamore (Rhine Tribunal) during apprenticeship, later Durenmar

Parens: Tiberius of Verditius (status: unknown after disgrace)

Current Status (1205): Recently relocated from Durenmar to Bergen shipyards, preparing to join Sjórseiðr as founding member

Childhood in Schleswig (1161-1171)

Torsten Magnusson was born in Schleswig, a bustling port town at the cultural and political border between Danish and German territories. His father Magnus was a shipwright of some reputation, his mother Margaret managed the household finances and ran a small chandlery supplying ships. The family was comfortable - not wealthy, but secure in the skilled craftsman class that formed the backbone of maritime trade.

From early childhood, Torsten showed aptitude for mechanical problems and spatial reasoning. By age five, he was "helping" his father at the shipyard - mostly getting underfoot, but already fascinated by how wooden pieces fit together to create vessels that could survive ocean storms. By seven, he was genuinely useful, his small hands perfect for reaching into tight spaces during construction.

Around age eight, his Gift manifested; the manifestation of the Gift brought the usual social consequences - sudden discomfort from friends, wariness from neighbors, his mother's barely-concealed fear. But his father, practical to the core, recognized opportunity. Magnus had heard stories of magi who crafted magical items, and reasoned that if his son must be different, he might as well be prosperously different. Through shipyard connections and trade networks, he learned of House Verditius and their reputation for craftsmanship. A message was sent; a response eventually came.

Apprenticeship at Triamore (1171-1186)

Finding Tiberius

Tiberius of Verditius came to Schleswig in person to assess the boy, drawn by reports of spontaneous metal-mending. A magus of middle years with a reputation as an excellent teacher but erratic political judgment, Tiberius saw potential in young Torsten's combination of practical experience and raw magical talent. The apprenticeship terms were negotiated: Torsten would train at Triamore in the Rhine Tribunal, where Tiberius maintained a laboratory. The boy's father extracted a promise that Torsten would learn shipbuilding alongside magic - a promise Tiberius kept, recognizing that the hands-on knowledge would complement Verditius methods.

Torsten arrived at Triamore at age ten, leaving behind his family but bringing his father's shipwright tools - a gift meant to remind him where he came from. The covenant, with its emphasis on vis trade and practical magic, suited his temperament better than a more scholarly environment would have.

Training Under Tiberius

Tiberius proved an excellent but demanding parens. His teaching philosophy combined rigorous Hermetic theory with practical application - every principle had to be demonstrated in the laboratory, every spell tested until it worked reliably. This approach suited Torsten perfectly.

From Tiberius - Hermetic Training:

  • Verditius mysteries and the integration of craft with magic
  • Arts, with emphasis on Creo, Rego, and the Forms most useful for item creation (Terram, Aquam, Herbam)
  • Laboratory technique, with particular focus on precision and reliability
  • The philosophy that magic should work - not just theoretically, but practically and repeatedly

From Covenant Culture:

  • Trade craft and diplomacy - Triamore's emphasis on vis exchange taught practical negotiation
  • The importance of reputation and reliability in magical commerce
  • How to navigate Tribunal politics (which Tiberius was admittedly not great at, but tried to teach)

From Continuing Craft Study: Tiberius honored his promise. Torsten spent significant time during his apprenticeship continuing to learn shipbuilding, carpentry, and metalworking from local craftsmen. By his Gauntlet, he was genuinely skilled at both mundane and magical crafting - unusual even for House Verditius, where many magi focus purely on enchantment rather than physical construction.

Development of Maritime Focus

Torsten's specialization in maritime enchantment emerged organically from his background. Unlike most Verditius magi, Torsten came from a maritime craft family and never lost that connection. During his apprenticeship, he began to recognize a gap: the Order had extensive magical support for land-based activities (buildings, roads, agriculture) but relatively little for seafaring.

This struck him as absurd. Ships carried the Order's trade goods, connected tribunals across water, and enabled everything from vis exchange to Tribunal attendance. Yet maritime magical items were rare, often unreliable, and typically created by magi with no actual understanding of how ships worked.

Tiberius encouraged this specialization. He had his own interests (precision clockwork and mechanical astronomy) but recognized that maritime magic represented an underserved niche where Torsten could make genuine contributions. Together they began experimenting with enchantments adapted to shipboard conditions - accounting for salt water, variable weather, constant motion, and the need for items that could be operated by non-Gifted sailors.

Venus's Blessing and Social Complications

Despite his somewhat brusque manner and Pre -1, Torsten discovered during adolescence that he attracted romantic and sexual attention far more than his personality or appearance warranted. This confused him initially - he was aware he wasn't conventionally charming - but eventually recognized it as some quirk of his Gift or nature.

Unlike characters where Venus's Blessing creates pure problems, for Torsten it created complications:

  • Attention from inappropriate sources (particularly married women)
  • Misunderstandings about his intentions
  • Jealousy from rivals who thought he was manipulating affections
  • Difficulty forming genuine romantic connections because he could never be sure if attraction was real or magically influenced

He learned to be direct almost to the point of rudeness, hoping to discourage unwanted advances through sheer bluntness. This worked sometimes, but not always. By his Gauntlet, he'd accepted that this was simply part of his nature and channeled the attentions into necessarily brief affairs.

The Disgrace of Tiberius (1185)

Torsten's fifteenth year of apprenticeship (when he was twenty-four, just before his Gauntlet) brought disaster. Tiberius became embroiled in a Verditius house dispute over precedence in a particular enchanting technique. The details were technically complex, but the politics were brutal. Tiberius, convinced he was right and that his rival was taking credit for his innovations, made increasingly rash decisions:

  • Public accusations at a House meeting
  • Demands for formal investigations
  • Eventually, an ill-conceived challenge that backfired spectacularly when evidence he'd relied upon proved fabricated (not by him, but by a supposed ally who betrayed him)

The scandal destroyed Tiberius's reputation. He wasn't formally Marched - his magical crime wasn't severe enough - but his standing in House Verditius collapsed. By late 1185, he left Triamore in disgrace, destination unknown. Some said he went into self-imposed exile at a remote covenant, others claimed he'd left the Order entirely.

For Torsten, this was devastating but also defining. He was so close to his Gauntlet that the covenant allowed him to complete it under supervision of another magus in 1186 (at age 25, older than typical due to Tiberius's extended apprenticeship period). But he entered full membership under a cloud: his parens was infamous, his training suspect, his future uncertain.

Infamous Master (Minor Flaw)

The taint of Tiberius's disgrace followed Torsten into his career. Other Verditius magi viewed him with suspicion: Was he involved in his parens's schemes? Did he inherit Tiberius's poor judgment? Could his work be trusted, or would it embarrass those who associated with him?

This reputation was grossly unfair - Torsten had nothing to do with the political disaster - but House Verditius ran on reputation and perception. He gained a Reputation (level 3) among magi: "Apprentice of disgraced Tiberius." Even magi who didn't know the details knew the name and the general story.

Journey to Durenmar (1186-1198)

Early Independence

After his Gauntlet, Torsten faced a choice. Triamore, while not hostile, made clear they couldn't offer him permanent residence - the political complications of his connection to Tiberius were too significant. He needed to establish himself somewhere his work could speak for itself.

He spent two years (1186-1188) as an itinerant magus, traveling the Rhine Tribunal and taking commissions for enchanted items. This was difficult - his Infamous Master reputation made magi hesitant to hire him - but he learned valuable lessons:

What worked:

  • Demonstrating actual skill through test pieces before negotiation
  • Underbidding more established enchanters (he needed the work more than the profit)
  • Focusing on practical items that served clear needs rather than prestige pieces
  • Building relationships with companion-level characters (ship captains, merchants) who cared about function, not gossip

What he discovered:

  • He genuinely enjoyed solving practical problems more than creating impressive showpieces
  • Maritime clients were underserved and less concerned about Hermetic politics
  • His shipbuilding knowledge gave him advantages other enchanters lacked
  • He was good at this - reliably, consistently good in ways that gradually built positive reputation

Maritime Specialization Deepens: During these itinerant years, Torsten took every maritime commission he could find. He created waterproofed containers, wind-finding devices, ship bells that could signal across miles, fresh-water producing barrels, and countless minor enchantments for navigational instruments. Each project taught him more about the specific challenges of maritime magic:

  • Saltwater's unique magical properties and how they interfered with standard enchantments
  • The importance of durability in items subjected to constant environmental stress
  • How to design for operation by non-Gifted users with minimal training
  • The value of redundancy and fail-safes in life-or-death applications

Move to Durenmar (1188)

In 1188, opportunity emerged. Durenmar, the domus magna of House Bonisagus, had a long-standing practice of hosting magi from other Houses who could contribute to research and scholarship. Torsten, through a contact made during his itinerant period, learned that Durenmar's library contained extensive theoretical texts on aquatic and aerial magic, but few practical applications.

He proposed a residency: he would work from Durenmar, use their library to develop his maritime enchantment theories, and in exchange provide the library with laboratory texts and theoretical treatises on practical maritime applications. Durenmar, always hungry for new knowledge, agreed. The arrangement was mutually beneficial:

For Durenmar:

  • Access to a growing body of practical maritime magical knowledge
  • Laboratory texts for useful but previously unexplored applications
  • A resident enchanter available for commissions
  • Prestige from patronizing a promising young specialist

For Torsten:

  • Exceptional library resources for theoretical research
  • Political protection - being based at Durenmar conferred status
  • A stable laboratory for long-term projects
  • Distance from the most intense Verditius house politics

The arrangement wasn't full covenant membership - Torsten remained technically independent, paying for his residency through lab texts and occasional commissions. But it gave him stability and resources he desperately needed.

Building Reputation (1188-1198)

Over his decade at Durenmar, Torsten methodically rebuilt his reputation through simple excellence. He:

Produced Reliable Work:

  • Every commission completed on time and to specification
  • Laboratory texts documented with unusual clarity and precision
  • Theoretical treatises that balanced rigorous Hermetic theory with practical application
  • A growing collection of innovative maritime enchantments

Developed Theoretical Framework: Using Durenmar's library, Torsten integrated his practical experience with serious theoretical study. He wrote treatises on:

  • "The Unique Properties of Salt Water in Hermetic Applications" (examining how Aquam effects behave differently with sea water versus fresh water)
  • "Designing for Motion: Enchanting Items Aboard Moving Vessels" (addressing the challenges of maintaining magical effects in constantly shifting environments)
  • "Redundancy and Reliability in Life-Critical Maritime Enchantments" (arguing for design philosophy prioritizing function over elegance)

These works gained respect in the Order. They weren't revolutionary - Torsten wasn't trying to overturn Hermetic theory - but they filled genuine gaps in magical knowledge. His combination of theoretical rigor and practical application impressed scholars.

Avoided Political Entanglements: Learning from Tiberius's mistakes, Torsten stayed scrupulously neutral in House Verditius politics. When rivals traded accusations, he kept working. When competitions demanded participation, he submitted technically excellent but politically neutral pieces. When asked to take sides, he demurred politely and returned to his laboratory.

This wasn't cowardice - it was strategic. He recognized that his Infamous Master reputation made political involvement dangerous. Better to let his work speak for itself.

Nocturnal Work Patterns: Torsten developed into a dedicated night worker, most productive between sunset and dawn. His laboratory at Durenmar operated on an inverted schedule - he slept during the day (when social demands were highest anyway) and worked through the night. This suited his temperament: fewer interruptions, quieter conditions, uninterrupted concentration. It also meant he was often absent from daytime covenant social functions, which helped him avoid political complications.

Growing Frustration (1198-1204)

Despite his success, Torsten grew increasingly frustrated with Durenmar and House Verditius politics:

Durenmar's Limitations:

  • The library was excellent, but the covenant itself was intensely conservative
  • Every innovation faced layers of review and political consideration
  • Practical applications were valued less than theoretical elegance
  • His maritime work was respected but considered somewhat eccentric - interesting, but not serious scholarship

Verditius Politics:

  • Despite his careful neutrality, he couldn't escape house politics entirely
  • Rivals still referenced his connection to Tiberius when convenient
  • Competition-focused culture valued prestige pieces over practical items
  • Increasing pressure to participate in political factions
  • Growing sense that function-over-form approach was seen as minor talent rather than deliberate philosophy

The Fundamental Problem: Torsten realized he was building his career on someone else's foundation. At Durenmar, he would always be "the maritime specialist in residence" - valued but never central, respected but never influential. In House Verditius, he would always be "Tiberius's apprentice" - the stain gradually fading but never entirely gone.

What he wanted was the chance to establish something from scratch, where his methods and philosophy could be foundational rather than supplementary. He wanted to prove that practical, reliable magic designed for real-world use was just as valuable as prestigious showpieces. But at Durenmar, that was impossible.

He began to feel trapped by his own success. He'd built a good reputation, but within a system that fundamentally didn't value what he valued. And he couldn't see a way out that wouldn't mean starting over yet again, in his late thirties, with even fewer resources.

Meeting Solving (1197)

Initial Correspondence

Torsten first encountered Solving of Criamon's name in 1197, when a mutual acquaintance mentioned an unusual Criamon mystic with strong theoretical knowledge of Aquam and Auram magic. This alone wasn't especially interesting - many magi knew weather magic - but the description of Solving's approach caught his attention: apparently, the Criamon treated magical theory almost as engineering problem-solving rather than pure mysticism.

Out of curiosity, Torsten wrote to introduce himself and inquire about a theoretical problem he was wrestling with: how to account for saltwater's magical properties when enchanting ship components that would be constantly exposed to spray and storm conditions. He expected either mystical nonsense or academic theorizing disconnected from practical concerns.

Instead, Solving's response was remarkably useful. The Criamon approached the problem systematically, drawing on Hermetic theory but also on natural philosophy and even alchemical principles. His explanation provided genuine insights that helped Torsten refine his approach. The letter concluded with questions about Torsten's practical experience with maritime enchantments - Solving seemed genuinely curious rather than dismissive.

A correspondence developed. Initially focused on technical problems, it gradually expanded:

Their Intellectual Dynamic:

Torsten's approach: "I need X to work reliably in Y conditions. Current methods fail because of Z. How do I solve this?"

Solving's response: "Let's examine the underlying theoretical principles. According to Hermetic theory... but also consider natural philosophy's perspective... which suggests three possible approaches..."

Torsten's reaction: Tests all three, reports which works best and why

Solving's follow-up: "Interesting! That suggests the theoretical model should be adjusted thus..."

This was novel for both of them. Torsten had worked with theorists before, but they rarely cared about practical validation. Solving had corresponded with craftsmen before, but few had the theoretical grounding to meaningfully engage with his analyses. They complemented each other perfectly:

  • Solving's high Intelligence and Magic Theory (5) provided sophisticated theoretical frameworks
  • Torsten's practical experience and strong Arts (particularly Aq 10, Au 7, Te 10) allowed rigorous testing
  • Solving's Enigmatic Wisdom sometimes produced intuitive leaps that bypassed problems Torsten was stuck on
  • Torsten's insistence on actual results kept Solving's theorizing grounded in reality

Solving's Visit to Durenmar (1199)

When Solving and his parens Lucretius visited Durenmar in late 1199 as part of a research trip through the Rhine Tribunal, Torsten made time to meet them in person. The visit went exceptionally well:

They spent two days in Torsten's laboratory, examining his maritime enchantments and discussing both successes and failures. Solving brought fresh perspectives, Torsten demonstrated practical techniques. Lucretius, observing the interaction, remarked that it was rare to see a Criamon and Verditius communicate so effectively - usually the philosophical differences proved insurmountable.

What struck Torsten most was that Solving treated him as a colleague, not as "Tiberius's apprentice" or "the maritime specialist." The Criamon seemed entirely uninterested in Hermetic politics or reputation - he cared whether Torsten's ideas were interesting and his work reliable. This was refreshingly different from Durenmar's subtle status hierarchies.

They discussed Epicurean philosophy (which Torsten found interesting but not personally compelling - he was too driven for ataraxia), debated whether magical innovation came from theoretical insight or practical necessity (they agreed it required both), and discovered a shared frustration with magical communities that valued prestige over function.

The Vision Shared (1201)

In early 1201, Solving wrote with an unusual proposal. He'd experienced a vision (typical for Criamon, though Solving's were reportedly more concrete than most) of a powerful vis source in the North Sea. More significantly, he was considering attempting to found a mobile covenant - ships rather than a fixed location - designed to access maritime magical resources that land-based covenants couldn't reach.

This was an audacious idea, probably impossible, and exactly the kind of challenge Torsten had been craving.

Solving's letter was characteristically direct: "I need someone who understands both maritime conditions and magical enchantment at a level far beyond my capabilities. I can theorize about what such a covenant would require. You could actually create it. Would this interest you?"

Torsten's response was immediate: "Yes. What do you need?"

Developing the Vision (1201-1204)

Theoretical Collaboration

Over the next three years, Torsten and Solving engaged in intensive correspondence about the practical requirements for a mobile covenant. This collaboration refined both their thinking:

Torsten's Practical Questions:

  • How do we prevent enchanted items from being destroyed by storms?
  • What happens to laboratory equipment during rough seas?
  • How do we maintain a library on ships (moisture, salt, mold)?
  • How do we ensure fresh water, food preservation, basic survival?
  • What about vis storage, magical security, defense against pirates or sea monsters?

Solving's Theoretical Responses:

  • Drew on his Bonisagus and Criamon training to provide theoretical frameworks
  • Suggested applications of Hermetic magic Torsten hadn't considered
  • Raised philosophical questions about what a covenant fundamentally needed (challenging Torsten to think beyond traditional models)
  • Connected maritime magic to broader Hermetic theory

Their Evolving Design: The correspondence produced detailed designs for essential magical items:

  • The Master's Chart (requiring collaboration with a cartographer-mage)
  • Bell of Summoning (fleet communication)
  • Brooch of Lungs of the Fish (underwater capability)
  • Freshwater Barrels (solving basic survival)
  • Magical Astrolabes (navigation and magical detection)

Each design went through multiple iterations as Torsten identified practical problems and Solving helped solve them theoretically. The process itself was deeply satisfying for both - this was exactly the kind of practical-theoretical synthesis both valued.

Growing Conviction

As the designs developed, Torsten's commitment deepened. This wasn't just an interesting project - it was the opportunity he'd been waiting for. Specifically:

Escape from Durenmar: The mobile covenant offered escape from Durenmar's conservative environment without abandoning magical society entirely. He wouldn't be isolating himself; he'd be founding something new.

Beyond Verditius Politics: A mobile covenant in a different tribunal would physically separate him from House Verditius's political drama. The distance alone would be liberating.

Ground Floor Opportunity: This was what truly excited him: the chance to establish standards and practices from the beginning. No existing traditions to navigate, no entrenched interests to accommodate, no "we've always done it this way" blocking innovation. They would create protocols for maritime magical research, establish what worked, document everything properly, and build something that functioned correctly from day one.

Proving His Philosophy: He could demonstrate that practical, reliable magic designed for real-world use was not only viable but superior to prestigious showpieces. The covenant's survival would depend on his enchantments working consistently under harsh conditions - exactly the kind of challenge that vindicated his entire approach to magic.

Study Requirement and Practical Planning

Torsten's Study Requirement flaw (major) shaped his research approach significantly. He was unable to study magic from books or vis alone - he needed to study in the presence of the appropriate Art's physical manifestation:

  • Aquam: needed access to water (preferably the sea)
  • Terram: needed metal, stone, earth
  • Herbam: needed living plants or significant wood
  • Creo: needed things growing or being created
  • Rego: needed things being moved or controlled

This wasn't usually a major handicap at Durenmar, but it did mean his study patterns were unusual. He needed to conduct Aquam research at the riverbank, Terram studies in workshops surrounded by metal and stone, Herbam work in gardens or near forests. This reinforced his practical, hands-on approach - he literally couldn't divorce magical study from physical reality.

For the mobile covenant, this would actually be advantageous. Living aboard ships would provide constant access to water for Aquam study, wind and weather for Auram, the ship's wooden structure for Herbam, metal fittings for Terram. His Study Requirement would be naturally accommodated by the maritime environment.

The Fall of Constantinople (April 1204)

News of Constantinople's sack reached Durenmar in late spring 1204. For Torsten, this was primarily significant because of its impact on Solving. The letters from Solving after the disaster carried a new urgency - his Criamon companion had experienced the vision's validation (the catastrophe he'd foreseen) and now felt compelled to pursue the North Sea vision immediately rather than waiting for optimal conditions.

Torsten understood the impulse. Sometimes you had to act, even if conditions weren't perfect. Solving had saved lives by trusting his vision; now he trusted another vision about the North Sea. That kind of conviction was rare and valuable.

Moreover, Solving wrote that he'd fled Constantinople with a Byzantine cartographer named Photios Chrysoloras, and that Photios would be essential for creating The Master's Chart - the cornerstone enchantment for the mobile covenant. This added another piece to the puzzle: not just Solving's theoretical knowledge, but Byzantine magical cartography integrated with Hermetic enchantment. The collaboration was becoming richer and more complex.

Torsten wrote back with commitment: he would relocate to Bergen (the shipyards Solving had identified as optimal for ship construction) and begin the practical work of preparing vessels and creating the essential enchanted items. This required significant personal disruption:

Leaving Durenmar:

  • Dismantling and moving his laboratory
  • Concluding obligations and commissions
  • Extracting his laboratory texts and research materials
  • Negotiating his departure (amicable, though Durenmar wasn't thrilled to lose a productive resident)

Relocating to Bergen (1204-1205):

  • Establishing temporary laboratory facilities at the shipyards
  • Beginning work on enchanted items while ships were under construction
  • Learning about Nordic ship design (different from German/Mediterranean vessels)
  • Hiring craftsmen and beginning the practical work of outfitting vessels

By early 1205, Torsten was established in Bergen, deep in the work of creating the magical infrastructure for Sjórseiðr.

Personality and Motivations (1205)

Core Traits

Ambitious (+3): Torsten wants to be recognized as a master craftsman whose practical approach to magic is not just valid but superior to prestige-focused enchanting. He's not pursuing power or status for their own sake - he wants vindication for his philosophy. The mobile covenant represents his chance to prove that reliable, functional magic designed for real-world conditions is the highest form of the art. This ambition drives his work ethic and shapes his goals, but it's focused and specific rather than grandiose.

Overconfident (Minor Flaw): Torsten has complete faith in his own technical judgment. When he designs an enchantment, he knows it will work because he's tested every component, validated every principle, and built in appropriate redundancies. This confidence is usually justified - he is genuinely skilled - but it makes him dismissive of concerns he considers unfounded. If someone questions whether his design will hold up in storm conditions, he'll explain (perhaps too patiently) exactly why his calculations are correct. He doesn't conceive of his work failing; if something goes wrong, there must be external factors or user error, not flaws in his design.

Busybody (Minor Flaw): For someone who deliberately avoids political entanglements, Torsten is remarkably well-informed about what's happening in magical communities. He keeps tabs on House Verditius developments, tracks news from various tribunals, knows which magi are feuding and which are collaborating. He's genuinely interested in this information - it's not just gossip, it's professional intelligence about potential collaborators, rivals, and opportunities. He subscribes to the Hermetic equivalent of trade newsletters, maintains correspondence with dozens of contacts, and generally knows what's happening in magical crafting circles across Europe. The covenant will find him a valuable source of information about the wider Order, even if he insists he's "just keeping professionally informed."

Nocturnal (Minor Flaw): Torsten's natural rhythms keep him sleeping until midday and working through the night. He suffers -1 on all rolls between dawn and midday, making him sluggish and irritable during early morning. This works surprisingly well for laboratory work - he has uninterrupted nighttime hours for delicate enchanting. It's less ideal for covenant business meetings or social obligations, which tend to happen during his worst hours. He tries to schedule important work for afternoon and evening when possible. His nocturnal habits reinforce his somewhat isolated social position - he's often asleep when others are most active, and most active when others are sleeping.

Methodical and Patient: In laboratory work, Torsten is extraordinarily patient. He'll test an enchantment repeatedly, make incremental adjustments, validate every component, and only declare success when he's certain of reliability. This methodical approach extends to problem-solving generally - he breaks complex challenges into manageable components, addresses each systematically, and builds comprehensive solutions rather than clever quick fixes. This makes him excellent at sustained projects but sometimes frustratingly slow at improvisation or emergency responses.

Protective of His Reputation: Having spent years overcoming the stigma of his parens's disgrace, Torsten guards his reputation carefully. He's scrupulously honest about his capabilities, delivers on every promise, documents his work thoroughly, and avoids anything that might be construed as sharp practice or corner-cutting. This makes him reliable but also somewhat rigid - he'll refuse commissions he's not certain he can complete perfectly, even when a partial solution might be useful. He's also sensitive (more than he'd admit) to suggestions that his work is somehow suspect or inferior.

Verditius Magic and Philosophy

Torsten's approach to Verditius Magic emphasizes the integration of mundane crafting skill with Hermetic enchantment:

Craft-Magic Synthesis: He genuinely believes that the best enchanted items emerge from deep understanding of the underlying craft. You can't create optimal ship-protecting wards without understanding naval architecture. You can't enchant navigation instruments without knowing how sailors actually use them. This philosophy shapes both his item creation and his criticism of other Verditius magi who know theory but not craft.

Function Over Form: While Verditius Magic traditionally values artistic beauty, Torsten prioritizes functionality. His items are well-made but not ornate, elegant in their efficiency rather than their decoration. He argues (sometimes contentiously) that the most beautiful enchantment is one that works perfectly, every time, under the worst possible conditions. Aesthetic embellishment is acceptable if it doesn't compromise reliability; otherwise it's vanity.

The "Right Way" Philosophy: Torsten has strong opinions about proper design methodology:

  • Test thoroughly before deployment
  • Build in redundancy for critical functions
  • Design for the worst-case scenario, not optimal conditions
  • Document everything so others can replicate and improve
  • Never compromise reliability for elegance or speed

He can be insufferable about this, particularly when others take shortcuts he considers unacceptable. "That's not how we should be doing this" is one of his frequent refrains. The mobile covenant gives him the opportunity to establish these principles as foundational practice rather than fighting existing traditions.

Major Magical Focus

Torsten's Major Magical Focus is maritime construction and enchantment - specifically magic as applied to ships, nautical equipment, and the maritime environment. This encompasses:

  • Enchanting ship components (hulls, masts, sails, rigging)
  • Creating maritime navigational devices
  • Protective wards for vessels and crew
  • Items designed to function in marine conditions (salt water, storms, constant motion)
  • Magic involving the interaction of wooden ships, metal fittings, canvas sails, and water

When working within this focus, he adds his lowest applicable Art score twice to his totals. This makes him extraordinarily effective at his specialty while remaining normally capable in other areas. For example, enchanting a ship's bell to amplify sound across miles of ocean would benefit from the focus, while creating a bell for a covenant's tower would not.

His focus doesn't extend to all water magic (pure Aquam effects without maritime context don't qualify) or all construction (building houses doesn't count). It's specifically the intersection of vessels, equipment, magical enchantment, and the maritime environment.

Inventive Genius and Skilled Parens

Despite Tiberius's eventual disgrace, he was an excellent teacher who provided Torsten with thorough training. The Skilled Parens virtue reflects both the quality of instruction and the extra time Tiberius devoted to ensuring his apprentice's success. Combined with Torsten's natural Inventive Genius (+3 to Lab Total when not using texts, +6 when experimenting), this makes him exceptionally effective at creating new enchantments.

However, his Cautious with Magic Theory virtue reveals another dimension - he doesn't trust inspiration alone. He uses his inventive capabilities to generate possibilities, then rigorously tests them before implementation. When others might experiment recklessly, he experiments carefully, rolling two fewer botch dice. His genius lies in systematic exploration rather than wild inspiration.

Deficient Form: Vim

Torsten's magical aptitude is uneven. While extremely capable with the Forms used in item creation (Terram, Aquam, Herbam), his understanding of Vim is poor. All totals involving Vim (except Magic Resistance) are halved, making him weak at:

  • Detecting or analyzing magic (InVi)
  • Dispelling or counter-magic (PeVi)
  • Transferring or manipulating magical energy (ReVi)
  • Understanding fundamental magical principles (raw Vim)

This is more than a minor inconvenience - it means he struggles with meta-magical effects that many magi take for granted. He can't easily analyze enchantments he encounters, has difficulty with magical investigation, and would be nearly useless in magical combat requiring Vim spells. His Magic Resistance is normal (based on Form scores), but he can't supplement it with Vim effects.

This deficiency reinforces his practical focus: he creates items that work through straightforward application of Hermetic theory rather than clever meta-magical solutions. It also means he relies on other magi (like Solving with his strong Vim) for magical investigation and analysis.

Current Motivations (1205)

Building the Foundation: Torsten is focused on creating the magical infrastructure Sjórseiðr needs to function. This includes:

  • The essential enchanted items (Master's Chart, Bell of Summoning, etc.)
  • Establishing laboratory protocols for ship-board research
  • Training crew and magi in proper use and maintenance of magical equipment
  • Documenting everything so the work can be replicated and improved

This practical focus drives his day-to-day activities and will for the covenant's first several years.

Proving the Philosophy: Every successful deployment of his enchantments, every crisis averted by reliable magical items, every time his practical approach prevents disaster - these vindicate his entire philosophy. He's building a track record that demonstrates practical magic's superiority. Success at Sjórseiðr will make his reputation and validate his approach.

Establishing "The Right Way": As a founding member, he has the opportunity to establish best practices from the beginning. He's writing protocols for:

  • Laboratory safety procedures for ship-board research
  • Proper maintenance schedules for enchanted items
  • Testing and validation procedures for new enchantments
  • Documentation standards for laboratory texts
  • Training procedures for non-Gifted users of magical items

These protocols reflect his conviction that magical communities fail when they rely on individual brilliance rather than systematic excellence. Get the systems right, and even average magi can do exceptional work.

Avoiding Tiberius's Mistakes: Torsten is consciously building a career that won't collapse the way his parens's did. This means:

  • Staying out of political vendettas (focus on work, not feuds)
  • Building reputation through demonstrable results rather than political maneuvering
  • Maintaining relationships based on mutual professional respect
  • Never letting emotion override judgment in important decisions

He's aware that his Ambitious and Overconfident traits make him vulnerable to some of the same problems Tiberius faced. He compensates through methodical approaches and by surrounding himself with people who'll call out his excesses (he values Solving partly for this reason - the Criamon doesn't care about his ego and will disagree when necessary).

Finding Community: Despite his solitary work habits and nocturnal schedule, Torsten wants to be part of something meaningful. Durenmar never felt like home - he was always a resident, not a member. Sjórseiðr offers the chance to build community from the ground up, where his contributions are foundational rather than supplementary. He wants to be known as one of the founders of something significant, part of a group that accomplished what others thought impossible.

This isn't about friendship per se (he's not especially warm or social), but about belonging to an endeavor larger than himself. The mobile covenant gives him purpose beyond individual achievement.

Relationship with Solving

Torsten's relationship with Solving Epicurusson is professionally based but has developed into genuine friendship:

Intellectual Equals: They approach magic from different angles - Solving's theoretical/philosophical, Torsten's practical/applied - but both are rigorous and systematic. Neither talks down to the other, and both regularly challenge each other's assumptions productively. Their correspondence has made both better magi.

Complementary Weaknesses: Solving's low Dexterity and lack of craft knowledge means he needs someone who can translate theory into physical reality. Torsten's Deficient Vim and limited theoretical breadth means he needs someone who can provide sophisticated theoretical frameworks. They genuinely need each other's expertise.

Trust in Vision: Torsten doesn't fully understand Solving's Enigmatic Wisdom or his visions, but he trusts Solving's sincerity and judgment. When Solving says he's experienced a premonition, Torsten takes it seriously - not because he believes in mystical prophecy, but because Solving's track record (fleeing Constantinople before the sack) validates his intuitions. Solving saved lives; that earns credibility.

Different Goals, Shared Purpose: Solving is pursuing his vision of the North Sea vis source with mystical conviction. Torsten doesn't share this spiritual motivation - for him, the covenant is about proving his practical philosophy and building something properly. But their different motivations lead to the same destination, and each respects the other's reasoning even when not sharing it.

Practical Dynamic: In practice, their relationship involves:

  • Regular consultation on technical problems
  • Solving asking "theoretically, could we...?" and Torsten responding "practically, yes if..." or "no because..."
  • Torsten presenting design challenges and Solving helping solve them theoretically
  • Mutual veto power on bad ideas (Solving can flag when Torsten's overconfidence leads to risky designs, Torsten can identify when Solving's theories aren't physically implementable)

They're not emotionally close in the way of intimate friends - neither is particularly demonstrative - but they're deeply reliant on each other professionally and have developed genuine affection through years of productive collaboration.

Relationship with Other Sjórseiðr Members

Photios Chrysoloras (Byzantine Cartographer-Mage): Torsten initially views Photios as a collaborator on The Master's Chart project - someone with specialized knowledge necessary for a key enchantment. Their working relationship develops slowly, complicated by:

  • Cultural differences (Nordic/Germanic practicality vs. Byzantine sophistication)
  • Different magical traditions (Hermetic Verditius vs. Jerbiton)
  • Language barriers and different communication styles
  • Torsten's tendency to steamroll collaborators with his "right way" philosophy

However, creating The Master's Chart requires genuine collaboration. Neither can complete it alone. This forces them to learn each other's methods, find common ground, and eventually develop mutual respect. By the time the Chart is complete, they've formed a working partnership that transcends their initial differences. Photios appreciates Torsten's engineering precision; Torsten respects Photios's theoretical sophistication about magical cartography.

Khalida al-Zahra (Flambeau Weather-Mage): Torsten's relationship with Khalida is professionally cordial but not especially close. They're both practical magi focused on maritime magic, which creates natural common ground:

  • Khalida needs weather-working spells, which Torsten's items complement
  • Torsten's enchantments require understanding of weather conditions, which Khalida can explain
  • Both value function over prestige, making them philosophical allies

However, personality differences limit deeper friendship:

  • Khalida's tempestuous emotional intensity vs. Torsten's methodical calm
  • Her self-protective recklessness vs. his careful reputation management
  • Her mystical Sufi faith vs. his practical, somewhat materialist worldview
  • She's constantly in motion; he prefers sustained focus on single projects

They work well together professionally and would defend each other in covenant politics, but they're not natural friends. Torsten finds her exhausting; she finds him plodding. Both recognize the other's competence and value, which is sufficient.

Crew and Companions: Torsten works closely with non-Gifted crew members and companions, particularly those who operate his enchanted devices. His background as a shipwright's son makes him comfortable with craftsmen and sailors in ways many magi aren't:

  • He explains device operation clearly and patiently
  • He values their feedback about practical use
  • He'll adjust designs based on user experience
  • He treats skilled sailors and craftsmen with genuine respect

This makes him popular among the covenant's mundane population, who appreciate that he creates items they can actually use and maintains them reliably.

Venus's Blessing Complications

Torsten's Venus's Blessing creates ongoing social complications despite (or because of) his Pre -1 and relatively brusque manner:

Inappropriate Attention:

  • Noble women at ports finding excuses to tour the ships
  • Sailors' wives or daughters showing interest he doesn't reciprocate
  • Political complications when important peoples' spouses or children become infatuated

Relationship Difficulties: When Torsten genuinely finds someone attractive, the Blessing creates doubt: is their interest real, or magically influenced? This makes authentic romantic connections nearly impossible. He's had a few relationships over the years, but they've all foundered on this uncertaint

Mitigating Strategies:

  • Being almost rudely direct to discourage misunderstanding
  • Mentioning non-existent wife/betrothed when necessary
  • Working at night when social interactions are minimized
  • Keeping laboratory and living quarters clearly private
  • Having allies (Solving particularly) run interference

Positive Applications: The +3 bonus on Communication and Presence with sexually compatible characters does occasionally help in negotiations, though Torsten is uncomfortable deliberately using it. When buying materials from a female merchant or negotiating with a male noble, the bonus can smooth interactions. He tries not to think about why it's working.

Faerie Friend

Torsten's Faerie Friend is a minor complication and occasional benefit:

Identity: Isolde Docksdottir (name she uses when dealing with humans; her true name is something else)

  • Appears as a young woman in her early twenties, though she's much older
  • Associated with harbors, ships, and maritime trade
  • Trickster nature - helpful but mischievous
  • Can speak when she chooses, but often communicates through actions
  • Has minor powers related to ships and water

Relationship: Isolde attached herself to Torsten during his years as an itinerant enchanter, apparently fascinated by someone who combined mundane shipwright skills with magical abilities. She appears unpredictably:

  • Sometimes provides useful information about ships, weather, or maritime conditions
  • Sometimes "helps" in ways that create as many problems as they solve
  • Occasionally steals small items from his workshop, only to return them in inconvenient locations
  • Has saved him from serious trouble at least twice (once from drowning, once from a dangerous maritime spirit)

Torsten has a frustrated but genuine affection for her. She's infuriating, but her help has been valuable, and he's learned to work around her mischief. He suspects she followed him to Norway and will show up at Sjórseiðr eventually, causing chaos in creative ways.

Complications:

  • Her "help" sometimes makes situations worse before better
  • She has her own agenda that doesn't always align with his
  • Other magi may not appreciate a faerie hanging around the covenant
  • She knows things about Torsten he'd prefer kept private
  • Her presence can attract other faerie attention

multipleCasting.sumary: Int + Finesse + stress die - no of spells VS 9

basicLabTotal.sumary: Int + Magic theory + Aura (+ Technique + Form)

totalPenetration.sumary: Penetration + (Active effects penetration like Arcane connection or Sympathetic Connection)

sigil.label: Wizard's Sigil
sigil.value: A sharp ringing in the ears of those nearby.

system.description:
The Gift is a special Virtue, because it has no cost. The character suffers all the penalties of The Gift, just as magi do (see page @@), but can be taught Supernatural Abilities without having to take the corresponding Virtues (see page @@ for rules). Most importantly, the character can be taught Hermetic Magic, so all magi must have this Virtue. A character with The Gift, even if he is not a magus, may take Hermetic Virtues and Flaws which relate to intrinsic ability rather than background or training. Characters may take Virtues that grant Supernatural Abilities without taking The Gift, and such characters do not suffer the penalties imposed by The Gift. However, such characters may not learn new Supernatural Abilities in the course of the saga, although they may improve the ones they already have. It is possible for unGifted characters to Initiate new Supernatural Abilities through a Mystery Cult, but even this is harder for them than for the Gifted.

Characters who have The Gift may start play with a single Supernatural Ability, without having to take any other Virtue, but if they wish to learn others they must find opportunities to do so in the course of the saga. They may also take Virtues granting Supernatural Abilities if they wish to have more Abilities at character creation. Note that it is harder for a character with Supernatural Abilities to become a Hermetic magus (see Training Your Apprentice on page @@), so you may not wish to take any such abilities if you plan for the character to become an apprentice. The ability to cast Hermetic magic is the single supernatural ability possessed by Hermetic magi in virtue of The Gift again, they may take more Supernatural Ability Virtues if they wish.

Grogs can never have The Gift, as a character with The Gift is too important to be a grog. As a rule, companions should only have The Gift if they are intended to become magi, or if they are members of one of the other magical traditions described in Ars Magica 5th Edition supplements. Troupes should not allow any other Gifted characters unless they are absolutely sure that they want them.
You are a member of the Order of Hermes. All magi must take this as their Social Status, and only magi may take it.

Your magic is much more potent in a fairly limited area, such as weather, necromancy, birds, or emotions. This area should be smaller than a single Art, but may be spread over several Arts – necromancy, for example, covers both Corpus and Mentem effects. You cannot be focused on laboratory activities, although a focus does apply to laboratory activities.

 

Sample Major Magical Foci :

This list is merely exemplary. Other foci are possible.

 Animal: Birds, or mammals, or domesticated animals.

 Aquam: Salt water or fresh water.

 Auram: Weather.

 Corpus: Men or women.

 Herbam: Trees or food plants.

 Imaginem: Visual images, auditory images, tactile images, or taste and smell together.

 Mentem: Emotions or memories.

 Terram: Metals or stone.

 Necromancy: Corpus and Mentem as applied to the bodies and spirits of dead people.

 Damage: Any Art, as long as the effect does damage directly, either by inflicting a wound or doing a certain amount of damage which can be soaked.

 Disguise: Changing the appearance of something, by any means.

 When you cast a spell or generate a Lab Total within your focus, add the lowest applicable Art score twice. If a spell has requisites, the lowest applicable score may be one of the requisites, rather than one of the primary Arts. Thus, if a magus with a focus on birds was casting a spell to turn a bird into pure flame, MuAn (Ig), with Muto 14, An 18, and Ig 10, his final total would be 34 + other modifiers: 14 from Muto, and 20 from adding Ignem twice. If he was casting a spell to turn a bird into another sort of bird, MuAn with no requisites, his total would be 46 + other modifiers, 18 from Animal and 28 from adding Muto twice.

 A character can have only one Magical Focus, either major or minor, regardless of the source of the focus.

Invention comes naturally to you.

You get +3 to your Lab Total if you are not using a Laboratory Text or being taught. If you experiment, you get +6.

Your parens was more powerful, or a better teacher, than is common. You gain an additional 60 experience points and 30 spell levels during apprenticeship. This Virtue has no effect on your study after apprenticeship, but you still have a close relationship with a powerful magus.

You have been initiated into the Outer Mystery of Verditius Magic (see page @@), and thus are a member of House Verditius. Note that all Verditius magi gain this Virtue for free at character creation.

People are often attracted to you. You get +3 on Communication and Presence rolls with sexually compatible characters in appropriate situations. At times you can put this to good use. At other times it’s an annoyance.

Once per day, when in need, you can perform an incredible feat of strength. For the duration of the action, add +3 to your effective Strength score. Afterwards, though, you must make two Fatigue rolls.

You are particularly adept with one Ability, and add 2 to its value whenever you use it. Note that you do not, in general, use an Ability when learning it, writing about it, or helping someone else to improve it. You may only take this Virtue once for a given Ability, but may take it more than once for different Abilities.

You have an additional three points to spend on buying Characteristics, but you are still limited to a maximum score of +3 in any single Characteristic unless you take the Virtue Great Characteristic. You may take this Virtue multiple times.

You are very careful with a specific Ability, and are less likely to fail spectacularly if you do fail when using it. You roll two fewer botch dice than normal whenever you are required to roll botch dice for that Ability. This may mean that you roll no botch dice. This Virtue may apply to any Ability, even one you cannot learn at character creation.

All Advancement Totals for one Ability are increased by half, rounded up, as are any experience points you put in that Ability at character creation. You may only take this Virtue once for a given Ability, but may take it again for different Abilities. If you take this Virtue for an Ability, you may exceed the normal age-based cap during character generation (see page @@) by two points for that Ability.

You are unable to study magic from books or vis alone. You must study in the presence of the appropriate Art. For example, you need to sit next to a brook or pond to study Aquam, or a large fire to study Ignem. Growing things are good for Creo, decaying ones good for Perdo. As your knowledge grows, you need to work with larger and larger quantities. See the Study Bonus Virtue on page @@ for a list of examples.

You may take both Study Bonus and Study Requirement.

Almost all totals (including Casting Totals and Lab Totals, but excluding Magic Resistance) to which a particular Form is added are halved. Advancement Totals are not halved. Experience points required are based on the actual value of the Technique, before halving.

You want to be the most successful or powerful person in the world in some respect. You will not be distracted into doing things that do not contribute to your ambition, and are very eager to do things that advance it.

You usually know everything that is going on among your friends and acquaintances, especially in private matters. You are always interested in rumors and gossip, and are frequently able to extract personal secrets from people. Among magi the language is more dignified, but the idea is the same: you keep tabs on the Hermetic community (your covenant, your House, and nearby covenants — at the least). However, magi probably don’t have much knowledge of what’s going on among the lower-class members of their covenant unless they choose to apply this Flaw specifically to such people at character creation.

You have a completely unshakable faith in your own abilities. You believe that you will always perform at, or slightly above, the peak of your abilities, and cannot imagine failure. If you actually botch, you come up with some rationalization as to what 'really' happened.

You have an ally among the fay. How much assistance you can get depends on the power of the friend — a small faerie with (relatively) minor powers can accompany you on a day to day basis. Powerful faeries have other business, and may only be available to answer questions and provide guidance, not to accompany you around on your adventures. Your ally is fully sentient, has its own powers, and can speak when it chooses. You may want to have another player act the part. While a faerie companion can be a great boon, it can also be a terrible burden. If it is nasty or mischievous, it could cause trouble for you everywhere. Characters with this Flaw can purchase the Arcane Knowledge Faerie Lore, even if they are normally restricted from purchasing it.

Your natural body rhythms try to keep you sleeping until noon. You are at –1 on all rolls made between dawn and midday. Conversely, you have little difficulty staying up at night. Though you enjoy no special benefits in the dark, your companions may decide to saddle you with night guard duty on a regular basis to use your attributes to best effect.

Your master was a diabolist, bumbler, fool, widely despised, or held in contempt for some other reason. Most magi expect little better from you. Even if you’ve done nothing wrong, you are treated as though you don’t deserve to be a member of the Order. You have a bad Reputation of the appropriate type at level 3 among magi.

Knowledge of one particular region, covenant, or even a village. It includes knowing where things are in the immediate area, local history and legends, and the centers of power in the region. The smaller the region, the more detailed your knowledge.

Specialties: geography, history, legends, politics, personalities. (General)

Care and use of animals, including raising, tending, grooming, and healing them.

Specialties: falconry, specific animals. (General)

General physical prowess. It includes moving smoothly, confidently, and with grace. Use of Athletics improves most large-muscle coordination.

Specialties: acrobatics, contortions, grace, jumping, running. (General)

The know-how to get the greatest return for a service or product, while paying the least. It involves reading the person with whom you haggle, a general knowledge of the value of goods, and presenting yourself in certain ways. A good haggler can easily overcome resistance in an inexperienced customer and see through attempts to over-value most merchandise.

Specialties: specific types of products, hard sell. (General)

The ability to enjoy oneself without suffering adverse effects. A person with this skill can, for example, consume prodigious amounts of alcohol without passing out. The skill also covers a familiarity with acceptable behavior, balancing enjoyment and moderation. With this Skill, a person is able to have fun and gain friends among the lower classes nearly anywhere, even among those of a different culture.

Specialties: power drinking, drinking songs, games of chance, staying sober. (General)

Enticing, fascinating, and endearing others to you, but only on a personal basis. It can be used to win someone over emotionally, especially members of the opposite sex.

Specialties: courtly love, first impressions, being witty. (General)

Focusing your mental faculties on one task, particularly for extended periods of time. If you are attempting a feat that demands your extra attention, or if you have just failed an action and are trying again, the storyguide can call for a Concentration roll before you can make the attempt. This Ability is especially important for magi because it helps them maintain concentration on spells despite distractions.

Specialties: spell concentration, reading, lab work. (General)

A general term for countless Abilities, all dealing with handiwork of some type. In general, Craft Abilities are distinguished by the material they work with, although you may also take a Craft Ability that allows the character to work with several materials in one specific way. You may purchase Craft more than once, choosing a different one each time. Skill in one Craft does not imply skill in any others. City and Guild contains more detailed information about Craft Abilities and their use, in Chapter 4.
Specialties: as appropriate to the craft. (General)

A general term for countless Abilities, all dealing with handiwork of some type. In general, Craft Abilities are distinguished by the material they work with, although you may also take a Craft Ability that allows the character to work with several materials in one specific way. You may purchase Craft more than once, choosing a different one each time. Skill in one Craft does not imply skill in any others. City and Guild contains more detailed information about Craft Abilities and their use, in Chapter 4.
Specialties: as appropriate to the craft. (General)

A general term for countless Abilities, all dealing with handiwork of some type. In general, Craft Abilities are distinguished by the material they work with, although you may also take a Craft Ability that allows the character to work with several materials in one specific way. You may purchase Craft more than once, choosing a different one each time. Skill in one Craft does not imply skill in any others. City and Guild contains more detailed information about Craft Abilities and their use, in Chapter 4.
Specialties: as appropriate to the craft. (General)

You know the social graces and how to behave in different situations. Etiquette differs from Charm in that Etiquette covers proper behavior, rather than charming behavior. You cannot seduce someone with Etiquette, but neither can you get through an audience with the bishop on Charm alone.

Specialties: nobility, court, peasants, faeries, the Church. (General)

Setting snares, choosing appropriate hunting spots, and following and identifying the spoor of creatures of all varieties. This Ability also lets you cover your tracks or not leave any in the first place.

Specialties: tracking, covering tracks, hunting a specific animal. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

Description

0

Point and grunt

With one or more experience points, you know “please”, ”thank you”, and a few other words.

1

Basic questions and answers

“Where is the church?,” “Do you sell food?” Constant mistakes, and an atrocious accent. People must speak slowly and often repeat themselves, and you cannot string a conversation together.

2

Basic conversation

You can sustain a short conversation on a common topic. You still make many mistakes, and often fail to catch what others say.

3

Haltingly functional

You can hold a conversation on everyday topics, although it takes time, you make many mistakes, and your accent is still bad.

4

Functional

You can hold a conversation on non-technical topics, and make few mistakes. People do not normally need to repeat themselves. This is the minimum level required to study from a book.

5

Fluent

You still have an accent if this is not your native language, but it is weak. You speak as well as most natives. This is the minimum level required to write a book.

6

Elegant

You choose your words well, and have no accent if this is not your native language.

Two dialects of the same language generally have a –1 penalty. If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

Description

0

Point and grunt

With one or more experience points, you know “please”, ”thank you”, and a few other words.

1

Basic questions and answers

“Where is the church?,” “Do you sell food?” Constant mistakes, and an atrocious accent. People must speak slowly and often repeat themselves, and you cannot string a conversation together.

You can sustain a short conversation on a common topic. You still make many mistakes, and often fail to catch what others say.

You can hold a conversation on everyday topics, although it takes time, you make many mistakes, and your accent is still bad.

4

You can hold a conversation on non-technical topics, and make few mistakes. People do not normally need to repeat themselves. This is the minimum level required to study from a book.

6

You choose your words well, and have no accent if this is not your native language.

Two dialects of the same language generally have a –1 penalty. If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

Knowledge of the legends, history, structure, operation, and goals of the specified organization. Organizations can be as large as the Church, or as small as a local craft guild. The smaller the organization, the more detailed your knowledge. Order of Hermes Lore is particularly important to most magi.

Specialties: personalities, history, politics. (General)

The ability to do a job which does not involve making something. Examples include jongleur, reeve, sailor, steward, teamster, and washerwoman.

Specialties: depend on the profession. (General)

Riding and controlling a horse, especially under stress.

Specialties: battle, speed, tricks. (General)

The ability to propel yourself through water quickly and efficiently.

Specialties: long distances, diving, underwater maneuvering. (General)

The seven artes liberales, or liberal arts, are the basis of medieval higher education. Everyone learns them at the universities before passing on to study other subjects. It would be very rare for a character to have a score in any other Academic Knowledge without a score of at least one in this.

The artes liberales are divided into two groups: the Trivium (grammar, logic, and rhetoric) and the Quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, and music). The emphasis at this period is on the Trivium, but both are taught. (Note that Artes Liberales as a whole is one Skill, not seven separate ones.)

Grammar concerns theoretical questions of the structure of languages, rather than the ability to use a specific language (that is covered by Speak skills). The authorities are Priscian and Donatus. Logic is the study of the syllogism, and other forms of reasoning discussed by Aristotle. It is the most important of the arts at this period. Aristotle is the authority, in his logica vetus and logica nova. Rhetoric is the study of the theory of rhetorical forms: knowledge of the sort of tricks that an orator can use, rather than the ability to use them. The authority is Cicero, especially his De Inventione.

Arithmetic is the ability to add and subtract, multiply and divide. The authority is Boethius, De arithmetica. Geometry is concerned with study of plane and solid figures, and the authority is Euclid, in his Elementa. It also deals with the study of light, and Euclid's Optica is the authority here. Astronomy is concerned with predicting the positions of the stars and planets, and the authority is Ptolemy, in the Libri Almagesti. Music is purely theoretical, the study of proportions and musical theory, not the ability to sing or play an instrument. The authority is Boethius, in De Musica.

Artes Liberales also covers the ability to read and write. For every point in the Ability, the character can read and write one writing system, providing that he knows a language which normally uses that system: the Latin alphabet, the Greek alphabet, the Arabic alphabet, and so on.

Specialties: grammar, logic, rhetoric, arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, music, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

This skill is similar to all other (Language) Abilities, but it is only available to educated characters. It grants the ability to speak a language which is no longer used as a native language in the areas the character has lived in. The most important example is Latin. All educated characters in the West know Latin, since without it you cannot learn any Academic Knowledges. It is also the common language of the Church and Order of Hermes. In other areas of the world, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew fill similar functions, although of these only Hebrew is a dead language.

Specialties: academic usage, Church ceremonies, Hermetic usage. (Academic)

There are three philosophies — natural philosophy, moral philosophy, and metaphysics. Aristotle is the auctor for all of them, in different books. Most scholars study the philosophies after the Arts but before going on to Law, Theology or Medicine. Natural philosophy is the study of the sub-lunar world. It roughly corresponds to today's science. The main texts are the Physica, De Meteorologia, and De historia animalium. Moral philosophy is the study of the proper way of human life. It covers ethics, politics, and economics — the texts are the Ethica, Politica, and Economica. Metaphysics is the philosophy of the fundamental nature of the world. The main text is Aristotle's Metaphysica. The theories of the Philosophiae are all basically correct in Mythic Europe.

Specialties: natural philosophy, moral philosophy, metaphysics, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

Fighting with a weapon used in one hand, possibly using a shield with the other. This includes lances used as intended from horseback.

Specialties: any one weapon or shield, which covers using that weapon with any shield or none, and that shield with any weapon. (Martial)

Judging events according to the Code of Hermes and the Peripheral Code. In addition to memorizing important precedents, this knowledge includes the practical side of enforcing Hermetic law - for example, knowing when to push for a vote or how to present an argument.

Specialties: Wizards' Marches, apprentices, mundane relations, Tribunal procedures, political intrigue. (Arcane)

Manipulating your spells and performing special feats with them. You would use this Ability to position objects delicately or to target spells.

Specialties: grace, precision, any one Form. (Arcane)

Knowledge of what magic is and how it works, used primarily in the laboratory. Magic Theory deals with the technical details of Hermetic magic; Magic Lore covers knowledge of magical things in general. Anyone can learn Magic Theory, if they have access to a teacher or book (normally represented by a Virtue), but it is little use to those without The Gift.

Specialties: inventing spells, enchanting items, a single Art. (Arcane)

Protection from magic. This is a special ritual (not a ritual spell) that takes about two minutes to perform. It lets you add 5 times your Parma Magica score to your Magic Resistance until the next sunrise or sunset, whichever comes first. You may suppress your Parma Magica temporarily by concentrating; this is equivalent to sustaining a Concentration duration spell. (See page @@). Parma Magica does not require concentration while it is active.

You may also protect one other person for each point in Parma Magica, with their consent. You must touch each person to start the protection, and it lasts as long as at least one character can see the other. The magus may cancel the protection at will, at any distance. While a magus is protecting others, his effective Parma Magica score is reduced by 3 points, both for himself and for the other people he is protecting. If the magus has a Parma Magica score of 3 or lower, his Parma Magica provides each character, including himself, with a Magic Resistance of 0. In the magus's case, this is added to his Form resistance, and a magus protected by another magus's Parma Magica may also add his Form resistance.

Any character protected by a Parma Magica, whether their own or someone else’s, is unaffected by the social penalties of other people’s Gifts. If a protected character is Gifted, their Gift still imposes social penalties on others, unless those others are also protected by a Parma Magica, or are unaffected by The Gift for some other reason.

Parma Magica can only be learned by Gifted characters, although they learn it as a normal Arcane Ability, not a Supernatural Ability. It is only known by Hermetic magi, as the Order enforces the 'Join or Die' choice rigorously on anyone who knows it, as well as declaring a Wizard's March on the magus who taught it. Parma Magica is the last thing an apprentice learns, being taught the final key to the Ability after he swears the Oath.

Specialties: protection from any specific Form. (Arcane)

Getting your spell through the target's Magic Resistance. See page @@ for detailed Penetration rules.

Specialties: any one Art. (Arcane)

Turns water into air as it enters your lungs, allowing you to breathe water as you do air. This spell puzzles Hermetic theoreticians, as it behaves like a dynamic container target despite not being a container target. All known attempts to create analogous effects have failed, even though analysis of this spell has not revealed any sign of non-Hermetic influence, or integrated experimentation. Most magi just point to it as an example of how Hermetic magic is not fully understood, and get on with their lives.
Makes water run off one object or creature, protecting the target and the target’s apparel from dampness. The spell is broken if the target is submerged in water.

You know precisely what tomorrow’s weather will be, and you gain a general impression about the weather for the week ahead. This intuition only applies to the place where you cast the spell, and is based on extrapolating current conditions, so it is occasionally wrong (precise, but inaccurate). Still, it would take something like an interfering magical being, or a volcanic eruption, to throw the prediction off.

A low wave is formed to propel a small boat slowly through the water. When casting this spell, you commonly make a pushing gesture. This spell, and other similar ones, pick out a particular current in the body of water and make it behave as desired, and thus have Individual Targets.

In Silva of Merinita's version of this spell, the wave occasionally drifts or bobs about, as if it is happy and playful.

A wall of wind roars away from you, starting up to 10 paces away and continuing until it dissipates naturally. The wind is confined by any existing barriers, but if unconfined it can be up to a hundred paces wide. All within the area must make a Dexterity + Size stress roll of 9+ or fall down and be blown along by the winds. The rolls must be made at the start of the gale and each subsequent round that the wind is maintained. You must concentrate on the gale. Missile fire into or out of the gale is futile, and marching against the gale requires a Strength + Size stress roll of 15+. Failure in this attempt mandates another Dexterity + Size stress roll of 12+ to keep from falling.
Grows 10 paces of vine from a moderate quantity of wood, or from fertile soil. The vine is extremely strong and pliable, suitable for use as rope.

Like Unseen Arm (ReTe 5), but it can carry large objects such as crates. Roughly speaking, it has the capabilities of a very strong person (Str +5). The heavier the object is, the slower the unseen porter moves. If delicacy is required, high Finesse stress rolls (12+ or so) are needed. The spell can only carry inanimate objects, and cannot carry you. It also cannot lift things more than 6 feet above the ground. Casting requisites of an appropriate Form for the target are required.
A 2-foot, thick, spear-shaped jet of fire flies from your palms, doing +15 damage to the individual it hits.
